Not really documentation, but it is somewhat.
YouTube video of program, which is also documentation-esque (has instructions): https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=z9-oAtb6ymw

## **Criterion C: Development**

Note that my code took a different route than what was said in Criterion A and B; I used the raw data dumps themselves instead of the Python Discogs client, as the data dumps are CC0 No Rights Reserved, while the API can have some restricted data (Discogs n.d.-a, n.d.-b), meaning there aren't any usage issues or rate limiting to deal with. 

**Please open/read the related file in the appendix when any specific file is mentioned. This is my ‘the appendix must be read if mentioned’.** 

In my program, I used a variety of languages: Bash, Python, HTML, and JavaScript. Bash was mainly used in shell scripts for the purpose of automating the update process, Python mainly for connecting to the database via SQLModel and processing requests to and from PostgreSQL, HTML for building an interface for database interaction (like forms) within the Shopify App Home Page, and Java mainly for listening for those forms and calling on the Python FastAPI for the response to that form data for display.**\[$\]** 

## **Non-Language-Specific Topics** 

### ***API*** 

As the Python module I used—shopifyapp—required the usage of an API (Shopify, n.d.-a), I chose FastAPI due to its documentation (FastAPI, n.d.), which was all I’ve ever wanted in life. This API also acts as the way to provide search results from the form, which can be seen from the following UML diagram (which is also the whole process of the app): ![][image1]Made with [diagrams.net](http://diagrams.net)

### ***File Management*** 

As should be seen in the appendix Directory Structure**\[$0\]**, there are quite a few files; I’ll discuss the reasoning for the files I was involved with placing. 

#### **App\[$1\]** 

All of the files involved with the program’s main searching function were placed here to keep them away from the rest. Multiple Python files were made for modularization and to prevent an absolute mess of the root directory. 

#### **Shell Scripts** 

setup.sh was placed in the root directory for obvious reasons;**\[$0\]** it’s not like it’s for setting up discogs-xml2db/postgresql/sql/ specifically. 

get\_latest\_dumps.sh is placed in discogs-xml2db/,**\[$2\]** as that is where the xml files will have to be. 

dumps\_to\_csv\_to\_psql.sh is placed in postgresql/,**\[$2.4\]** which is where the SQL-related files are contained. 

### ***Database Management | PostgreSQL*** 

Given the sheer quantity of data I had\[6\] and a need for speed,\[3.81\] I chose PostgreSQL as the project’s database (Google Cloud, n.d.). 

### ***discogs-xml2db\[$2\]*** 

For the purpose of database management, this GitHub repository was essential, as it does exactly as it is named: move the raw Discogs data dumps into a database (Mateescu, n.d.). One of those options was PostgreSQL, making the choice above even easier. This software is excellent for modularization, code reuse, and abstraction with its variety of Python and SQL programs that handle most of the hassle, and I only had to make a few modifications to fit the project’s purpose. 

### ***pip and npm*** 

Continuing the abstraction, code reuse, and modularization from before, a variety of packages were used, which should be seen from the requirements.txt**\[$6\]** and package-lock.json**\[$4\]** files. 

### ***GitHub*** 

To allow for the project to actually be developed further, I have created a GitHub repository for it, which is far more accessible than handing off a zip file that Barry would have to send to any other developer. 

## **Security** 

### ***Permissions*** 

Given that Barry makes use of Shopify to spread his music (Client, n.d.), and that my project acts as a search program while unextended, it would be pointless to request any sort of read-write permissions at the moment, for there is nothing to do with them; it would only be a security hazard (Shopify, n.d.-c) (the last time I saw Barry’s administration management, there were a variety of connections to the store nowhere near our location, so security is certainly for the best). Should the program require permissions, there are also files set up within the app directory to work with for that purpose, those being authenticate, convert\_request/response, and initialization.py,**\[$1.2-5\]** and PostgreSQL can also be used for this authentication management. 

### ***SQL Injection*** 

I believe I have done a variety of things to prevent SQL injection (though I doubt that there aren’t flaws); it would be truly awful to have to wait through transferring the files again. 

In sql\_query.py:**\[$1.8\]** 

* SQLAlchemy—and therefore SQLModel—sanitize their inputs (SQLModel, n.d.)   
* The dataclasses use Pydantic for data type confirmation   
* The query receives its conditions as a list of commands to be unpacked, not some f-string with user input (where\_conditions.append(getattr(table.c, k) \== v) 

In application.html:**\[$1.1\]** 

* When getting the form data, empty fields will be skipped over, so if someone queries with what would be a blank { } JSON, the query won’t go through (if (reqChildData.size \!== 0\) { . . . };) 

## **HTML and JavaScript\[$1.1\]** 

I don’t believe I can claim much for this that isn’t code reuse. Most of this was stitching code snippets together, learning from those code snippets to write some very basic HTML and JavaScript, and reading pieces of the documentation. I did make use of the Shopify Polaris elements instead of standard HTML elements to match the theme of Shopify, though that was most often switching elements in and out. 

To give credit: 

* Shopify’s *Shopify Dev Docs* for Polaris elements, code snippets, and instructions   
* User Avnish’s *Show/Hide a Form on button click using JavaScript* for the form format, showForm() function (from which I derived the JavaScript listed above the function)   
* A likely large quantity of the internet for the createTable function, as it made me suffer significantly, enough to ask ChatGPT   
* Walsh’s *How JavaScript Event Delegation Works* for the event listener   
* And likely most importantly, MDN’s *Learn web development* for explaining how a variety of elements work, API usage and cross-origin requests, methods, properties, and likely a variety of other things 

## **Bash and Shell Scripting\[$2.4.3; $2.10; $7\]** 

The backbone of the success criteria of automation lies here, specifically from the function provided by the top response to Stack Overflow user Jiaaro, which provides the directory to the setup.sh**\[$7\]** and therefore the location of every other file in the project directory, allowing setup.sh to run whatever it needs to. 

###  ***Echoes*** 

While being run, setup.sh will echo into the shell for the user to see; those echoes are either notices (e.g., current step or sudo usage in setup.sh), alerts (e.g., checksum verification failure in get\_latest\_dumps.sh**\[$2.10\]**), or instructions (like to change the password, which is a cat command, but it’s serving the same purpose as an echo). 

### ***Multiple Shell Scripts*** 

The two non-setup.sh scripts both act for modularization and abstraction, with them doing what their names say while being out of the way. Both get\_latest\_dumps and dumps\_to\_csv\_to\_psql.sh**\[$2.4.3\]** are called from setup.sh. 

### ***Various Pre-Installed Shell Commands (Not Echo)*** 

The scripts make use of commands, like retrieving output from a command with $(. . .) to get the user or date, exporting an environment variable for cross-script usage, substituting default values in text files, issuing commands via heredocs, and safely deleting old directories with rm \-r. 

### ***Various (probably) Self-Installed Shell Commands*** 

* python3: run various Python files; make virtual environment   
* pip: install various required Python packages from requirements.txt   
* wget: download the data dumps from [data.discogs.com](http://data.discogs.com) and output to files   
* sha256sum: confirm that the data dumps are not corrupted by using CHECKSUM.txt   
* psql: run commands related to database management; most of the credit for the EOF heredoc goes to the second top reply to Stack Overflow user EMP (“most” for the two lines that I wrote myself) 

## **Python\[$1.2-8\]** 

### ***Collections Usage*** 

Dictionaries and lists held a variety of roles in this project (modifying and accessing contents are excluded): 

In sql\_init.py:**\[$1.7\]** 

* All table objects are added to the globals() dictionary so that they can be accessed wherever sql\_init.py is imported 

In sql\_query.py:**\[$1.8\]** 

* A dictionary (class\_dict) of Search\_Params’ nested classes are created in a function called construct\_class\_init\_dict   
* A dictionary is created from the class Search\_Params, which is used for a for loop that adds to class\_dict   
* A list of conditions (where\_conditions) is made out of the above dictionary to be unpacked into commands   
* A list of dictionaries (requested\_items) is made from a list comprehension’s items’ mappings 

In main.py:**\[$1.6\]** 

* A dictionary/JSON (data) is received from application.html (FastAPI shifts sent JSON to a Python dictionary and vice versa)   
* class\_dict is keyed for a specific class (e.g., Artist), then has the dictionary *data* unpacked into it so that the dataclass now has an instance; that instance is fed to the function read\_items() of sql\_query.py 

Here’s a UML diagram to show the process of the collections: 

![][image2]

Made with [diagrams.net](http://diagrams.net)

### ***Classes*** 

Dataclasses were a perfect choice for storing the requested data; for one, they immediately handle \_\_init\_\_s, with those \_\_init\_\_s being able to be unpacked into to make an instance (like step five above); they are also supported by FastAPI (n.d), as said in their dataclasses section, tying into the rest of the API. Also, they’re called **data**classes. 

All other dataclasses were nested in the dataclass Search\_Param(eter)s; Search\_Params has its type of search—Artist, Label, Master, Release, and others should they be added—and that search type has the parameters. 

When one of the nested classes is initialized, that dataclass instance can be fed to other programs, making it an easy way to share data. 

### ***@cache*****\[$1.8\] ** 

It’s not the most interesting thing I used, but it’s more unique than saying I commented appropriately on my code, which I did on every file; the construct\_class\_init\_dict function caches its results to prevent resource waste. 

Note that the references include everything even if it wasn’t discussed in this document. For example, I did not discuss the SQLAlchemy documentation, but I still used it when writing metadata-related code in sql\_init.py, so I would like to cite it. The references also include those related to other criteria. 

Word Count (excluding “Note that”s, parenthetical citations, appendix references, UML crediting, and this; I have counted file names and underscore-connected functions/variables as one word): 

**References**  
Avnish. (2024, December 31). *Show/Hide a form on button click using JavaScript*. Dev Community. https://dev.to/askyt/showhide-a-form-on-button-click-using-javascript-1obm  
Barnett, B. (2023, July 25). Sed \- an introduction and tutorial by Bruce Barnett. In *Grymoire*. https://grymoire.com/Unix/Sed.html  
Bayer, M. (Ed.). (n.d.). *SQLAlchemy 2.0 documentation*. https://docs.sqlalchemy.org  
Client, B. (n.d.). *Junie B Jones Records* \[Store\]. http://juniebjonesrecords.com  
Discogs. (n.d.). *Discogs data* \[Data set\]. https://data.discogs.com  
Discogs (Ed.). (n.d.). *Home \- Discogs API documentation*. https://www.discogs.com/developers  
EMP. (2011, November 11). *Create PostgreSQL ROLE (user) if it doesn't exist* \[Online forum post\]. Stack Overflow. https://stackoverflow.com/questions/8092086/create-postgresql-role-user-if-it-doesnt-exist  
FastAPI (Ed.). (n.d.). *Learn*. https://sqlmodel.tiangolo.com/learn/  
Google Cloud. (n.d.). *What is PostgreSQL?* https://cloud.google.com/discover/  
     what-is-postgresql  
Gitignore. (n.d.). In *Git*. https://git-scm.com/docs/gitignore  
Jiaaro. (2008, September 12). *How do I get the directory where a Bash script is located from within the script itself?* \[Online forum post\]. Stack Overflow. https://stackoverflow.com/questions/59895/how-do-i-get-the-directory-where-a-bash-script-is-located-from-within-the-script  
Josh. (2024, February 28). *Managing and handling configurations in a Shopify app*. Medium. https://joshatflare.medium.com/managing-and-handling-configurations-in-a-shopify-app-f6b36ca2f21a  
Mateescu, P. (n.d.). *discogs-xml2db* \[Computer program\]. https://github.com/philipmat/discogs-xml2db/  
MDN. (n.d.). *Learn web development*. Mozilla. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn\_web\_development  
PostgreSQL (Ed.). (n.d.). *Documentation* (17th ed.). https://www.postgresql.org/docs/  
*Python3 Discogs client documentation*. (n.d.). https://python3-discogs-client.readthedocs.io/en/latest/  
Python Software Foundation. (n.d.). *Python 3.14.3 documentation*. https://docs.python.org/3/  
Shopify. (n.d.). *shopifyapp* (Version 0.1.3) \[Computer program\]. https://pypi.org/project/shopifyapp/  
Shopify. (n.d.). *Shopify development fundamentals*. Shopify Academy. https://www.shopifyacademy.com/path/shopify-development-fundamentals  
Shopify (n.d.). *Shopify dev docs*. https://shopify.dev/docs  
SQLModel (Ed.). (n.d.). *Learn*. https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/learn/  
Walsh, D. (2011, March 7). *How JavaScript event delegation works*. https://davidwalsh.name/event-delegate
